## Overview

Is raising too much capital dangerous for startups? Blitzscaling vs efficiency.

*This page was auto-generated by the disagreement detector. It clusters enriched entity pages into opposing camps based on keyword and sentiment analysis of their Key Views sections.*

## Raise Big / Blitzscale

### Garry Tan

> Counter to the prevailing narrative, Garry believes the expansion of early-stage capital is healthy. The key isn't how much capital exists — it's whether investors can identify the subset of founders who will build generational companies. His portfolio at Initialized demonstrates that disciplined seed investing in the right founders still produces outsized returns.

^[raw/transcripts/20vc/20vc-2019-04-01-20vc-initializeds-garry-tan.md]

## Beware Overcapitalization / Stay Lean

### Aaron Levie

> Levie discusses the dangers of raising too much capital as a startup. Over-funding can mask underlying product-market fit issues, lead to bloated teams, and create a culture where the urgency to find product-market fit is replaced by the comfort of a large bank account. He believes the most dangerous thing for a startup is having enough cash to avoid making hard decisions.

^[raw/transcripts/20vc/20vc-2022-02-11-20vc-boxs-aaron-levie-on-why.md]

### Ed Sim

> Drawing on 19+ years in early-stage SaaS, Sim outlines the repeatable playbook for building successful enterprise SaaS companies. He shares frameworks from his investments in category leaders like LivePerson (on Nasdaq) and GoToMeeting (acquired by Citrix), emphasizing that the fundamentals of enterprise sales, product focus, and capital efficiency remain timeless.

^[raw/transcripts/20vc/20vc-2015-11-30-20-vc-092-the-repeatable-play.md]
